content.full_text:
Fnrn Por-rrrcAl Pnecrrcss CoMMrssroN
428 J Street o Suire 620 o Sacranrenro. CA 9-5gl.l-2329
(916) 322-5660 r Fax (916) 322-0tt86
June 24, 2008
M. L. Silva-Manuel
P. O. Box 386
Grizzly Flats, CA 95636
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-08-099
Dear Ms. Silva-Manuel:
This letter responds to yollr reqLlest for advice regarding the conflict-of-interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act (the ,,Act").1
Becattse you do not seek advice regarding a specific govemmental decision, we
are providing yoll only informal assistance. Informal assistance does not provide the
reqttestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section
831 l4; Regulation 18329(c), copy enclosed.)
Additionally, we base our advice solely on the provisions of the Act and do not
address the applicability, if any, of other conflict-of-interest laws such as common law
conflict of interest, Government Code Sections 1090 and 1099, or your agency's
incompatible activities policy.
QUESTION
Will holding positions simultaneously with the Pioneer Fire District Board and
the board of your local firefi-ehters' association, a non-profit corporation, create a conflict
of interest?
CONCLUSION
Simtrltaneotrsly holding positions with the Pioneer Fire District Board and the
board of your local firefighters' association will not create a conflict of interest uncler the
Political Reform Act. However, yoLl may wish to contact yolrr agency's counsel or the
' The Political Refbrm Act is contained in Government Code Sedions ll 1000 throLrgh 9l0l.l. All
statutory ret'erences are to the Government Code. unless otherwisc'indicated. The rc,gulationi rlt the Fair
Political Practices conrmission are contained in Sections l8l l0 through 18g97 ot'Title I ()1'rhe clalifirrnia
Code of Regulations. All regulatory ref'erences arc'to Title 2. Dilision 6 of the Calitirrnia Cocle of
Regulations. unless otherwise indicated.


File No. I-08-099
Page No.2
Attomey General's Office with respect to the doctrine of "incompatible offices" and
other provisions of law that might apply to your facts. Also, you may wish to consult
yoLlr agency's counsel on whether holding a position on the board of the firefighters'
association conflicts with any agency policy on incompatible activities.
FACTS
You have recently been appointed as a director on the Pioneer Fire District Board
You are a former volunteer firefighter and serve as a director on the firefighters'
association board. You receive no stipend for serving on either board, and you have
minimal possibilities for being reimbursed out-of-pocket expenses. There ii no other
financial benefit to you.
You ask.whether simultaneously holding positions with the Pioneer Fire District
Board and the board of your local firefighters' a-ssociation will create a conflict of interest
under the Act' Specifically, you are concerned about whether you may have a conflict in
participating in district budget decisions regarding items that would r,-ipport volunteer
firefighters. This would include funds allocated for required personal piotective gear and
uniforms, funds for a per-call stipend for personal vehicle use in responding to
emergency calls, or funds that pay an expense stipend for volunteers to *oik a 24-hour
duty shift to supplement the paid crew. Because you are no longer a volunteer firefighter,
you would not be financially affected by any of the above budget items.
The firefighters' association is a non-profit corporation that mainly supports the
fire district through its fundraising efforts. The funds raised go toward ofisetting ttre
direct expense in the above budget areas. There are some voiunteer rights provided
through a Memorandum of Understanding.
You are being questioned by a community member who believes that you should
abstain from voting on anything to do with the district's budget due to what he perceives
as a conflict of interest.
You wish to know if serving simultaneously as a director of the firefighters,
association (giving money to the fire district to supplement their budget) and-as a director
of the local fire district (making the best possible decisions on how tlie district uses its
funds) present a conflict of interest.
ANALYSIS
Incompatible Offices
The Act does not prohibit a public official from holding multiple public posirions,
either within a single agency or different agencies. The confliit-of-iniere.st provisions of
the Act (Section 87100 et seq., discussed below) do prohibit a public official from


File No. I-08-099
Pa-ee No. 3
participating in a decision that will have a financial effect on his or her economic
interests. However, this prohibition is applied on a decision-by-decision basis, and
several exceptions apply.
Please note that other laws, outside the jurisdiction of the Commission, do restrict
the ability of public officials to hold two different pr.rblic offices simultaneously if the
offices are determined to be "incompatible offices.l' It would be advisable to ctntact
yollr agency's counsel or the Attorney General's Office with respect to the doctrine of
"incompatible offices" (Section 1099) and other provisions of law, including yor.rr
agency's own policy on incompatible activities that might apply to your racls.
Conflict of Interest
Your question implicates the Act's conflict-of-interest rules, which generally
prohibit public officials from making, participating in making, or using theii official
positions to influence the making of a governmental decision in which they have a
financial interest. The general rule, briefly stated, is that a conflict of inteiest exists when
a public official plays a decision-making role in a governmental decision that will have a
reasonably foreseeable, material financial effect on one or more of the official's
economic interests. The Commission uses an eight-step analysis to determine whether a
public official has a disqualifying conflict of interest, (Regulation 18700 (b)(l)-(g), copy
enclosed) which we outline to illustrate the analysis of a decision by the local iire district
board in light of the circumstances you describe.
Step L: Are you a public official?
The Act's conflict of interest provisions apply only to "public officials.,,
(Sections 87100, 87103; Regulation 18701.) a "puUlic oifi.iuli'is defined by rhe Act ro
include every "member, officer, employee or consultant of a state or local government
agency," with some exceptions not relevant here. (section g204g.)
"Local government agency means a county, city or district of any kind including a
school district, or any other local or regional political subdivision, o. ory department,
division, bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the foregtinj." (Section
8204L) As a member of the Pioneer Fire District Board, you u." a public official for
purposes of the Act.
step 2: will you be making, participating in making, or using your oflicial
position to influence a governmental decision?
The Act's conflict-of-interest provisions apply only where a public official
"make[sl, participatelsl in making or in any way atrempr[s] to use his [or herl official
position to influence a governmental decision in whictrhe [or shel knows or has reason to
know he [or shel has a financial inreresr." (Secrion g7100; Regulation 1g700(b)(2).) The


File No. I-08-099
Page No.4
Commission has adopted a series of regulations that define "making," "participating in
making," and "influencing" a governmental decision, which provide as iollowi:
o d public official "makes a governmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any collrse of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 1g702. l.)
A public official "participates in a governmental decision,'when, acting within
the authority of his or her position and without significant substantive &
intervening review, the official negotiates, advises or makes recommendations to
the decision maker regarding the governmental decision. (Regulation 18702.2.)
A public official is attempting to use his or her official position to influence a
decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or appears before
any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation
187023)2
a
Therefore, if you deliberate or vote on an item, commit your agency to a course of
action, enter into any contractual agreement, or use yollr official position to influence a
governmental decision, you will be engaging in activity regulated by the Act. (See
Regulation 18702.4.)
Step 3: What are your economic interests, the possible sources of a conflict-
of-interest?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a "financial interest" in a
governmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of tn" fficial's economic
interests, described as follows:
A public official has an economic interest in a business entity in which he or she
has a direct or indirect investment of $2,000 or more (Section g7103(a);
Regulation 18703.1(a)); or in which he or she is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, employee, or holds any position of management (Section g7103(d);
Regulation I 8703. 1(b));
a
a
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section g7103(b); Regulation
18703.2);
: Regulation 18702.4 (enclosed; lists a series of exceptions to the rules stated in the preceding
three re-eulations, which apply only in certain limited circumstances.


File No. I-08-099
Page No.5
o d public official has an economic interest in any solrrce of income, including
promised income, which aggregates to $500 or more within l2 months prior to the
decision (Section 87103(c); Regr-rlation 18703.3) "lncome" does not include
salary or per diem payments received from a local agency, or reimbursement for
travel expenses and per diem received from a bona fide nonprofit entity exempt
from taxation under Section 501(cX3) of the Internal Revenue Code. (section
82030(bx2));
o I public official has an economic interest in any source of gifts to him or her if
the gifts aggregate to $390 or more within l2 rnonths priorlo the decision
(Section 87103(e); Regulation lg703.4);
o Lastly, a public official always has an economic interest in his or her personal
finances, including those of his or her immediate family -this is the ..personal
financial effects" rule (Section g7103; Regulation 1g703.5).
You state that you do not receive income or "any stipend for serving on either
board," and you have "minimal possibilities for being reimbursed out of po""t "t
expenses." You also state that "[t]here is no other financial benefit to you."
Any salary and reimbursement for expenses or per diem received from a state,
local or federal govemment agency, or reimbursement ior travel expenses and per diem
received from a bona fide nonprofit entity exempt from taxatio, und". Section 501(c)(3)
of the Intemal Revenue Code, is expressly exempted from the definition of ,,income,, for
purposes of the Act. (Section 82030(bX2).)
Therefore, if you receive a salary or reimbursement for expenses from the fire
district board, a local govemment agency, these items would not be considered income.
Likewise, if you receive reimbursement for out-of-pocket travel expenses or per diem
from the firefighters' association board, and the board is a 501(c)(1 non-proiit, these
reimbursements would not be considered income and would not create a conflict of
interest with respect to decisions you would make on the local fire district Uoa.a.i
Assuming the decisions you make have no reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on your personal finances, it does not appear that you would have an
economic interest that could result in a conflict of intereit. Thereiore, the conflict
analysis ends at this point, with a conclusion that you do not have a conflict of interest in
budgetary decisions involving the firefighter's association board.
I Regardless of whether the firefighters' association is exempt trom taxation under Section
501(c)(3) of the Internal Revenue Code. if you receive reimbursemenis tiom the association tbr items other
than travel expenses these reimbursements may be considered income and could serve as the basis fbr
disqualification. If this is the case, you may request firrther advice.


File No. I-08-099
Page No.6
If you have other questions on this matter. please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
By: Emelyn Rodriguez
Counsel, Legal Division
ER:jgl
Enclosures
